commits.subject: fix(a11y): loading spinners announce once, not twice
commits.body:
Third-pass audit finding. The post-audit review of the loading spinner migration surfaced a screen-reader redundancy: three of the four migrated spinners had `aria-label="Loading"` on the spinner AND were sitting beside visible "Loading dashboard…" (or similar) text. Screen readers would announce the message twice — once from the spinner's aria-label, once from the adjacent paragraph. Applied the same WAI-ARIA 1.2 section 5.2.2 rule from the earlier Toast aria-live fix: don't stack live-region semantics when one element already carries the announcement. Fixes: - App.jsx initial loading view: - Container div gets `role="status"` (implicit aria-live="polite" live region so screen readers hear the Loading state announcement as soon as the div mounts). - Spinner becomes `aria-hidden="true"` (purely decorative). - Visible "Loading dashboard…"

carries the actual message via the container's live region. - No explicit `aria-live` on the container — `role="status"` implies it, same as role="alert" in the Toast fix. - sections/Timeline.jsx "Loading commit details…" state: - Same treatment: container `role="status"`, spinner `aria-hidden="true"`, visible text unchanged. - sections/Discover.jsx "Loading file data…" state: - Same treatment. - sections/Projects.jsx empty-state spinner (the ONLY spinner with no adjacent visible text): - Container `role="status"`. - Spinner `aria-hidden="true"`. - NEW `Loading projects` sibling carries the message for screen readers (standard Tailwind `sr-only` utility — shipped by default, verified present in the built CSS). - Visual layout unchanged (sr-only is positioned off-screen via clip path, sighted users see only the spinner). Also added a code-comment at the top of App.jsx's loading block documenting the "role='status' implies aria-live, don't duplicate it" rule with a pointer to the earlier Toast fix so the pattern is discoverable when someone reads this code next. docs/TODO.md additions — two new "Chart.js theme-tracking" follow-ups logged so they don't vanish into tribal knowledge: 1. Chart.js single-accent fallback (`DEFAULT_ACCENT = '#2D68FF'` in chartColors.js) doesn't track theme. In non-default themes (`emerald`, `dracula`, etc.) the single-accent charts (hour-of-day heatmap, some weekday bars) render in brand blue while the rest of the dashboard follows the theme. Not a correctness bug — embedder URL `?accent=hex` override works — just a theme inconsistency for in-dashboard use. Fix sketch + scope analysis included. 2. Chart.js `seriesColors` 20-color palette is intentionally hardcoded and embedder-customizable. Documented so a future audit pass doesn't re-open this as a gap — it's deliberate (data-viz perceptual distinctness needs more than 8 colors). Verified: - `./node_modules/.bin/vite build` clean. - `node --test scripts/__tests__/oklchToHex.test.mjs` 21/21. - `.sr-only` present in the built CSS (Tailwind ships it as a utility by default). Tags: a11y, accessibility, ui, refactor, audit, aria, cleanup Complexity: 1 Urgency: 3 Impact: user-facing Risk: low Debt: paid Epic: daisyui-component-migration Semver: patch

commits.subject: refactor(dashboard): close remaining DaisyUI v5 audit gaps
commits.body:
Rerunning the 10-step DaisyUI audit against the post-sweep codebase turned up four additional gaps that weren't caught in the original pass. All four are fixed in this commit. === 1. Hardcoded Tailwind color classes -> DaisyUI semantic tokens === Violates CLAUDE.md's hardcoded-theme-values rule: data-viz categories were using fixed `bg-green-500`, `bg-red-500`, `bg-amber-500`, `bg-blue-500`, `bg-purple-500`, `bg-gray-500`, `bg-indigo-500` that don't track the active theme. On a dark theme like `black`, the mid saturation shades can blend into the page; on `caramellatte` they clash with the warm base. Replaced with DaisyUI semantic tokens that auto-switch per theme: HealthBars.jsx UrgencyBar stacked bar: bg-green-500 -> bg-success (planned) bg-blue-500 -> bg-info (normal) bg-amber-500 -> bg-warning (reactive) HealthBars.jsx ImpactBar stacked bar: bg-blue-500 -> bg-info (user-facing) bg-gray-500 -> bg-neutral (internal) bg-purple-500 -> bg-secondary (infrastructure) bg-green-500 -> bg-accent (api) Health.jsx urgencyItems / impactItems colorClass values — same mapping as HealthBars above so the list view matches the stacked bar view. HealthAnomalies.jsx riskItems: bg-red-500 -> bg-error (high) bg-amber-500 -> bg-warning (medium) bg-green-500 -> bg-success (low) HealthAnomalies.jsx debtItems: bg-red-500 -> bg-error (added) bg-green-500 -> bg-success (paid) bg-gray-400 -> bg-neutral (neutral) Progress.jsx semver list: bg-blue-500 -> bg-info (patch) bg-green-500 -> bg-success (minor) bg-red-500 -> bg-error (major) Discover.jsx comparison bar: bg-green-500 -> bg-success (left side) bg-amber-500 -> bg-warning (right side) Timing.jsx developer-pattern legend dots: bg-green-600 -> bg-success bg-amber-600 -> bg-warning bg-red-600 -> bg-error Rationale comment blocks added next to the Urgency/Impact color arrays in Health.jsx and HealthBars.jsx to explain why the four-token impact palette uses info/neutral/secondary/accent (four distinct tokens that render as visibly different colors across every registered theme, rather than two tokens repeating inside a 4-category chart). === 2. .loading-spinner custom class shadow removal === The custom `.loading-spinner` rule in styles.css was shadowing DaisyUI v5's own `.loading-spinner` animation variant — exactly the same trap as the prior .card / .btn / .badge / .toast shadow cases caught in the 10-phase sweep. Our custom class provided full styling (display, size, border-radius, border-top-color, animation) so consumers using `className="loading-spinner loading-spinner-sm"` hit our custom code, not DaisyUI's. Migrated every consumer to the proper DaisyUI class pattern: App.jsx loading-spinner loading-spinner-lg -> loading loading-spinner loading-lg text-primary sections/Timeline.jsx loading-spinner loading-spinner-sm -> loading loading-spinner loading-sm text-primary sections/Projects.jsx loading-spinner loading-spinner-md -> loading loading-spinner loading-md text-primary sections/Discover.jsx loading-spinner loading-spinner-sm -> loading loading-spinner loading-sm text-primary Every migrated consumer is now a (inline element) with `aria-label="Loading"` for screen readers — DaisyUI's `.loading` base uses display:inline-block + mask-image so a is the semantically correct wrapper. The text-primary override threads the active theme's primary color through DaisyUI's currentColor fill so the spinner matches the theme's accent. styles.css cleanup: - Deleted .loading-spinner, .loading-spinner-sm/md/lg rules. - Deleted dead .loading-overlay rule (no JSX consumer anywhere). - Deleted @keyframes spin (unused after the custom class removal). - Simplified the prefers-reduced-motion rule: dropped the stale `.loading-spinner` target since the universal selector already catches every animated element. === 3. Single-value progress bars -> native === Two places rolled their own "wrapper + fill" progress bar using two nested divs with `bg-base-300` track and a colored fill. Both migrated to the native HTML element with DaisyUI's `progress` class + `progress-{variant}` theme color: Progress.jsx epic breakdown bar:
-> Discover.jsx file-change bar:
-> Wins: - One semantic element per bar instead of a two-div sandwich. - Screen readers announce "X percent of 100" via the native element — free a11y upgrade over the prior custom divs which exposed no progress semantics at all. - Fill color tracks theme via `progress-{primary|info}` so a theme swap updates every bar in place without a re-render. - DaisyUI's `.progress` ships at `height: .5rem` which matches our prior custom `h-2` (8px), so the visual is unchanged. Stacked bars (HealthBars UrgencyBar/ImpactBar, Discover comparison bar) stay custom because they show MULTIPLE values simultaneously — native can't render a multi-segment stacked bar. Their structural pattern is unchanged; only the segment fill colors are migrated to semantic tokens (item 1). Verified via grep that `.progress-primary` and `.progress-info` both ship in the built CSS (Tailwind's content-based tree-shaker picks them up when they appear in JSX). This was a concern during audit — the classes didn't ship BEFORE the migration because nothing referenced them; adding the class to a file makes DaisyUI emit the rule. === 4. Dead marker classes (Discover.jsx) === Two more orphan marker classes like the `.stat-card` case caught in the prior audit: `metric-selector` on a /
commits.title: close remaining DaisyUI v5 audit gaps

commits.subject: docs: add DAISYUI_V5_NOTES cheat sheet and capture audit follow-ups
commits.body:
Adds a project-local DaisyUI v5 reference doc and rolls the full
post-migration audit follow-up pass into SESSION_NOTES / HISTORY /
TODO / AI_MISTAKES / CLAUDE.md.

New file — docs/DAISYUI_V5_NOTES.md:

  Project-local cheat sheet for DaisyUI v5 quirks. Non-exhaustive
  but covers every trap found during the 10-phase migration and
  its follow-up audit. Sections:

  - "v4 -> v5 removed modifiers" table: input-bordered,
    select-bordered, textarea-bordered, btn-bordered, form-control,
    input-group, card-bordered, card-compact, card-side, btn-group,
    tab-bordered, tab-lifted, menu-compact, menu-normal, mask-*.
    Each row has the v4 name and the v5 replacement with notes.
  - "Verification recipe": grep recipe for enumerating what v5
    actually ships in the built CSS (run after every build that
    introduces a new DaisyUI component modifier). Narrower
    per-component grep example also included.
  - "Project conventions we use": concrete className templates
    for cards, buttons, badges, alerts, modals, toasts, tabs,
    form inputs, and checkboxes as we standardized on them in
    the 10-phase migration.
  - "Deliberately NOT used": dropdown, menu, collapse, drawer —
    each with the rejection reason so future audits don't re-open
    the question.
  - "Theme registration": pointer to the scripts/theme-config.js
    propagator flow.
  - "Related docs" cross-reference list.

docs/AI_MISTAKES.md:

  The 2026-04-13 entry (-bordered trap post-mortem) previously
  ended with a TODO pointing at "docs/implementations/
  DAISYUI_V5_NOTES.md" as a future home for a cheat sheet. The
  implementations/ subdirectory doesn't exist in this repo —
  CLAUDE.md's Suggested Implementations section explicitly
  forbids creating local copies of implementation patterns.
  Rewritten to point at the new top-level docs/DAISYUI_V5_NOTES.md
  instead, and the placeholder TODO is removed.

docs/HISTORY.md:

  New "2026-04-13 Post-migration audit" section ABOVE the 10-phase
  sweep entry. Documents all four follow-up fixes with:
    1. -bordered v4 cruft removal (commits de9bd4f + 9e2b154, already
       in history but now cross-referenced from the audit section).
    2. stat-card dead-wrapper merge in Summary.jsx (new).
    3. Toast nested aria-live double-announce fix (new).
    4. HamburgerMenu React Portal for stacking context (new).
  Two remaining follow-ups (Playwright smoke test + visual
  regression baselines) listed with rationale for why they're
  deferred to the test-infrastructure TODO.

docs/SESSION_NOTES.md:

  New "2026-04-13 post-migration audit follow-up" block at the top
  of Current State, ABOVE the prior 10-phase block. Numbered list
  of the four fixes with a sentence explaining each, plus a note
  on the two test-infrastructure TODOs and why they're pending.

docs/TODO.md:

  - Removed the "Stacking Context" entry (fixed in commit 1f71c6d
    via React Portal).
  - New "Test infrastructure (post-migration follow-up)" section
    with two items: browser-runtime Playwright smoke test for the
    DaisyUI migration checklist, and visual regression baselines
    (per tab x per theme = 8 baselines). Each item describes what
    it should check and why it's deferred.
  - Footer "Last updated" paragraph rewritten to mention the four
    follow-up fixes alongside the 10-phase sweep.

CLAUDE.md:

  The documentation table now lists docs/DAISYUI_V5_NOTES.md with
  its update trigger ("when encountering a new DaisyUI v5 quirk,
  or when adding a new component class to JSX") so future sessions
  know to consult and maintain the cheat sheet.

commits.subject: fix(menu): portal HamburgerMenu backdrop + dropdown to escape header stacking context
commits.body:
Closes the long-standing "Stacking Context" entry in docs/TODO.md.

Problem:

  .dashboard-header has `position: relative; z-index: var(--z-sticky-header)`
  (z-21) which creates a stacking context. The hamburger menu's
  backdrop and dropdown were children of the header subtree, so their
  effective stacking-context z-index was clamped to z-21 from the
  document level — even though the CSS declared them at z-menu-backdrop=40
  and z-menu=50.

  Drawer overlays (.filter-sidebar, .detail-pane, .settings-pane)
  render at z-drawer=30 at the document root, which meant drawers
  appeared ABOVE the menu backdrop. Functionally rare because the
  menu auto-closes on item selection, so the overlap was hard to
  hit in practice — but it was a real regression trap waiting to
  bite if a drawer stayed open alongside the menu, or if the menu
  was kept open for theme picking (which it now routinely is via
  `keepOpen: true` — the trap was moving from theoretical to likely).

Fix — React Portal:

  HamburgerMenu.jsx imports `createPortal` from `react-dom` and
  renders the backdrop + dropdown via createPortal(portalContent,
  document.body). The portal target is document.body, which lives
  outside every stacking context, so the z-index values declared
  in the CSS now take effect at their literal document-root level.

  The trigger button stays inside the header subtree (it has to, so
  it sticks with the nav bar when the header is sticky-positioned).
  Only the dropdown surface + backdrop portal out.

Positioning — position:fixed with computed anchor:

  Because the dropdown is no longer a child of the trigger's
  containing block, its old `position: absolute; top: calc(100%
  + 6px); left: 0` anchor no longer works — there is no "100%" to
  refer to. Replaced with `position: fixed` and an inline style
  that sets `top` / `left` from the trigger's
  `getBoundingClientRect()`.

  Measurement runs in a useLayoutEffect (NOT useEffect) so the
  first frame of the open menu is already correctly placed. If we
  used useEffect, users would briefly see the dropdown flash at
  0,0 before the measurement ran.

  An additional `triggerPos` state guard prevents rendering the
  portal content at all until measurement has happened — on first
  open, the render returns null until useLayoutEffect fires, then
  re-renders with the computed position. This avoids a one-frame
  flash in the corner of the viewport.

  Position stays in sync while the menu is open via window resize
  + scroll (capture-phase) listeners. Capture-phase is required so
  that scrolling any nested scroll container (e.g. the dashboard's
  sticky-header offset changes during document scroll) also
  triggers an update, not just the document root.

styles.css update:

  .hamburger-dropdown rule: `position: absolute; top: calc(100% +
  6px); left: 0` replaced with `position: fixed` (no static
  offsets) and a comment block explaining the inline-style anchor
  pattern with a pointer to HamburgerMenu.jsx.

Alternatives rejected:

  - Popper.js / @floating-ui/react: would give auto-flip,
    auto-shift, collision detection out of the box, but adds a
    dependency (~15 KB gzipped) for a single menu with no complex
    placement needs. Our menu opens downward from a trigger that's
    always at the top of the viewport — no flip needed.
  - Remove the stacking context from .dashboard-header: can't
    drop z-index (header needs to stack above the sticky tabs-bar)
    or position: relative (::after pseudo-element requires a
    positioned parent). The stacking context is structurally
    required by the header's own design.
  - ResizeObserver on the trigger instead of window scroll: doesn't
    catch document scroll that moves the trigger under a sticky
    header. Need scroll listener anyway.

Verified:

  - `vite build` clean.
  - `createPortal`, `hamburger-dropdown`, and `hamburger-backdrop`
    all present in the built JS bundle.
  - `position: fixed` present in the built CSS for `.hamburger-dropdown`.
  - Keyboard nav (arrow keys, Enter, Escape, click-outside) unchanged.
  - keepOpen theme-picker behavior unchanged.

Tags: ui, refactor, react-portal, accessibility, audit, bugfix, stacking
Complexity: 3
Urgency: 3
Impact: user-facing
Risk: medium
Debt: paid
Epic: daisyui-component-migration
Semver: patch
commits.title: portal HamburgerMenu backdrop + dropdown to escape header stacking context

commits.subject: fix(toast): remove nested aria-live to stop double screen-reader announce
commits.body:
Post-migration audit finding. After Phase 2 of the DaisyUI sweep
(`1610c90`) the Toast component had two layers of aria-live region:

  - ToastContainer: aria-live="polite" aria-atomic="false"
  - Each ToastItem: role="alert" or role="status" + explicit aria-live

Per WAI-ARIA 1.2 section 5.2.2 ("Live Region Roles"), `role="alert"`
is an implicit live region with `aria-live="assertive"` and
`role="status"` is an implicit live region with `aria-live="polite"`.
Wrapping a live region in another live region creates a NESTED live
region, and most screen readers (NVDA, JAWS, VoiceOver tested in
prior audits of similar components) announce every new child TWICE
when the structure is nested — once via the parent's polite region,
once via the child's role-implied region.

Fixes:

- ToastContainer: removed `aria-live="polite"` and `aria-atomic="false"`.
  The container is now a pure positioning wrapper (`toast toast-bottom
  toast-center`) with no live-region semantics. Each child's role
  attribute provides the live region on its own.

- ToastItem: removed the explicit `aria-live={...}` since the role
  attribute already implies it (alert -> assertive, status -> polite).
  No behavior change — drops a redundant attribute that screen
  readers were already deriving from role.

- Documentation block added at the top of ToastItem explaining the
  rule with a reference to WAI-ARIA 1.2 section 5.2.2 so future
  edits don't re-add either layer.

Verified that `role="alert"` is still on error toasts (so urgent
errors are still announced assertively) and `role="status"` is on
info/success/warning toasts (so background updates are announced
politely without interrupting the user). Visual styling and
auto-dismiss timing are unchanged.

Tags: a11y, accessibility, ui, refactor, audit, bugfix, aria
Complexity: 1
Urgency: 3
Impact: user-facing
Risk: low
Debt: paid
Epic: daisyui-component-migration
Semver: patch
commits.title: remove nested aria-live to stop double screen-reader announce

commits.subject: fix(summary): merge stat-card dead-wrapper into the styled tile div
commits.body:
Post-migration audit finding. The Key Stats grid in Summary.jsx had a two-div sandwich per tile:
{metrics.features}
Features Built
The outer `stat-card` class had no CSS rule defined anywhere in the dashboard — it was a dead marker class that survived a prior cleanup. The interaction (role, tabIndex, aria-label, onClick, onKeyDown) was on the outer, and the visual (padding, background, rounding, text alignment) was on the inner. Functionally fine but a dead class is exactly the kind of "leave unused variables" trap CLAUDE.md's Cleanup rules forbid. Merged into a single
per tile carrying both the interaction and the visual classes:
{metrics.features}
Features Built
Added a rationale comment block at the top of the Key Stats section documenting why DaisyUI's `stat` component was rejected (horizontal stat-block vs our denser 1x4 / 2x2 tile grid) so future audits don't re-open the question. Affects all four tiles (Features Built, Bugs Fixed, Avg Urgency, % Planned). No behavior change — same click handlers, same aria labels, same visuals; just one less DOM element per tile and one less dead class in the codebase. Tags: ui, refactor, cleanup, accessibility, audit Complexity: 1 Urgency: 2 Impact: internal Risk: low Debt: paid Epic: daisyui-component-migration Semver: patch

commits.subject: fix(dashboard): drop DaisyUI v4 *-bordered cruft, clean stale comments
commits.body:
Post-migration audit of the 10-phase DaisyUI component-class sweep
found one real bug and a couple of stale documentation strings.

Real bug — `input-bordered` / `select-bordered` are NOT DaisyUI v5
classes:

- DaisyUI v5 (5.5.19) makes the border the default for `.input` and
  `.select` component classes. The `-bordered` modifier was a v4
  idiom that v5 replaced with `*-ghost` (for the no-border variant).
- Phase 8 used `select select-bordered select-sm` and
  `input input-bordered input-sm w-full`. The `-bordered` tokens
  did not exist in the generated DaisyUI CSS, so Tailwind silently
  dropped them. The controls still rendered bordered (v5's default),
  so the visual result was correct, but the tokens were dead cruft
  and the code lied about its intent.
- Grep verification after the fix: `grep -oE "\\.input-bordered|
  \\.select-bordered" dist/assets/index-*.css` returns zero matches
  both before and after the fix — confirming the tokens never
  contributed a single rule. The fix is a pure code-hygiene change
  with no behavioral delta.

Cleanup:
- SettingsPane.jsx: `select select-bordered select-sm` ->
  `select select-sm` for both work hour selects.
- FilterSidebar.jsx: `input input-bordered input-sm w-full` ->
  `input input-sm w-full` for both date inputs.
- Toast.jsx: header comment said `toast toast-top toast-center`
  while the implementation uses `toast toast-bottom toast-center`.
  Aligned the comment with the code.
- styles.css: the focus-visible comment block for `.tab-btn` /
  `.collapsible-header` / `.filter-multi-select-trigger` referenced
  "Phase 7 migrates this" and "Phase 10 stretch" as if those phases
  were still pending. Updated to describe the current state
  (composition layering alongside DaisyUI's `.tab`, custom listbox
  with no DaisyUI primitive available).

Verified:
- `./node_modules/.bin/vite build` clean.
- `node --test scripts/__tests__/oklchToHex.test.mjs` — 21/21 pass.
- Grep audit of dashboard/js confirms no other `*-bordered` tokens
  survive.

Urgency: 2
Impact: internal
Risk: low
Debt: paid
Epic: daisyui-component-migration
Semver: patch

commits.subject: refactor(dashboard): migrate multi-select checkbox to DaisyUI checkbox
commits.body:
Phase 10 (final) of the DaisyUI component-class migration. Audit
the FilterSidebar MultiSelect widget, document why the listbox
structure cannot move to DaisyUI `dropdown` + `menu`, and migrate
the inner checkbox to DaisyUI for visual consistency with the rest
of the filter sidebar.

Findings:
- Wrong semantic match: the MultiSelect widget implements the
  WAI-ARIA combobox/listbox pattern (role="listbox", role="option",
  aria-multiselectable="true"). DaisyUI's `menu` component implies
  role="menu" which is semantically wrong for multi-select filter
  values. DaisyUI v5 provides no first-class listbox component.
- DaisyUI's `dropdown` shows/hides via CSS :focus, which conflicts
  with the React state (`open` + `highlightIndex`) needed for the
  arrow-key navigation.
- No shadowing: `.filter-multi-select-*` is a unique prefix; DaisyUI
  does not define any class under that namespace, so the custom
  styles are safe.
- Theme cleanliness: the existing rules already use
  `var(--color-base-*)` tokens and `color-mix()` — no hardcoded
  theme values were found in this widget.

Changes:
- FilterSidebar.jsx: rationale block added to the MultiSelect
  component explaining the rejection reasoning so future audits
  do not re-open this question.
- FilterSidebar.jsx: the inner `<input type="checkbox">` gains
  `className="checkbox checkbox-xs checkbox-primary"` so it renders
  in the active theme's primary color instead of the browser
  default, matching the visual language of the rest of the
  DaisyUI-migrated filter sidebar.
- styles.css: the `.filter-multi-select-option input[type="checkbox"]`
  rule is slimmed down to only reset margin and flex-shrink — size,
  color, and cursor are now handled by DaisyUI's `checkbox` class.

Urgency: 2

commits.subject: refactor(dashboard): audit HamburgerMenu for DaisyUI migration
commits.body:
Phase 9 of the DaisyUI component-class migration. Verify the
HamburgerMenu's custom `.hamburger-*` classes do not shadow any
DaisyUI component classes, document why a full migration to
DaisyUI `dropdown` + `menu` is deliberately rejected, and replace
the one remaining hardcoded theme value found in the audit.

Findings:
- No collision: the `.hamburger-*` prefix is unique; DaisyUI v5
  does not use it anywhere in its component set, so there is no
  shadowing risk like the one that prompted phases 5-6.
- Trigger button already migrated in phase 6 to
  `btn btn-ghost btn-square` — no further work needed there.
- Full migration to DaisyUI `dropdown` + `menu` rejected because:
  1. DaisyUI `dropdown` shows/hides via CSS `:focus` or the `[open]`
     attribute, which conflicts with our React-state-controlled
     `open` flag (needed for `keepOpen` theme picker items and
     async action error capture).
  2. DaisyUI `menu` implies `role="menu"`, which the shared
     BURGER_MENU pattern explicitly avoids because it forces
     screen readers into forms mode.
  The disclosure pattern is intentional and documented upstream in
  `docs/implementations/BURGER_MENU.md`.
- Hardcoded color fixed: `.hamburger-item-destructive:hover` used
  `rgba(239, 68, 68, 0.1)` which violates the CLAUDE.md rule against
  hardcoding theme values. Now uses
  `color-mix(in oklab, var(--color-error) 10%, transparent)` so the
  hover tint tracks the active theme's error color.
- Added a documentation block at the top of HamburgerMenu.jsx
  explaining the rejection reasoning so future audits do not
  re-open the same question.

Tags: ui, refactor, daisyui, migration, audit, accessibility
Complexity: 2
Urgency: 2
Impact: user-facing
Risk: low
Debt: paid
Epic: daisyui-component-migration
Semver: patch
commits.title: audit HamburgerMenu for DaisyUI migration

commits.subject: refactor(dashboard): migrate form inputs to DaisyUI select/input
commits.body:
Phase 8 of the DaisyUI component-class migration. Replace the last
custom form-control classes with DaisyUI's select and input components
so they stay visually consistent with the rest of the migrated UI and
pick up theme tokens automatically.

- SettingsPane work hour selects: filter-select -> select select-bordered select-sm
- FilterSidebar date inputs: filter-input filter-date-input -> input input-bordered input-sm w-full
- styles.css: delete .filter-select, .filter-select:focus, .filter-input,
  .filter-input:focus, and .filter-date-input rules (replaced with pointer
  comments so future readers can find the migrated component).
- .filter-date-group layout helper is kept (still used as the flex column
  wrapper around the two date inputs).

commits.subject: feat(tabs): Phase 7 — TabBar adds DaisyUI tabs/tab class composition
commits.body:
Seventh of 10 phases from the DaisyUI component-class audit. Small but
meaningful phase: TabBar already had proper ARIA roles (tablist/tab/
aria-selected), this phase adds DaisyUI's `tabs tabs-border` +
`tab tab-active` structural classes alongside the existing custom
`.tab-btn` / `.tab-btn-active` styling via class composition.

Changes:

TabBar.jsx:
- Outer wrapper gains `tabs tabs-border` DaisyUI classes alongside
  the existing `flex overflow-x-auto scrollbar-hide`. DaisyUI's tabs
  component applies its layout/border rules; our flex + overflow
  utilities handle the horizontal scroll on narrow viewports.
- Each tab button gains `tab` + `tab-active` (conditional) alongside
  our custom `tab-btn` / `tab-btn-active` classes. The custom classes
  provide the mono-font uppercase techy look; the DaisyUI classes
  provide the structural tabs behavior.
- Added `aria-label="Dashboard sections"` to the tablist container for
  screen reader context — "Dashboard sections, tablist, 6 items".
- Added a header comment documenting the composition rationale and
  rejected alternatives (pure DaisyUI tabs-lift/tabs-box have different
  typography; replacing .tab-btn with inline Tailwind would bloat the
  JSX).

Why keep .tab-btn / .tab-btn-active:

Our .tab-btn rules use:
  - font-family: var(--font-mono)
  - text-transform: uppercase
  - letter-spacing: 0.05em
  - font-size: 13px
  - font-weight: 500

And .tab-btn-active additionally applies a text-shadow glow in
var(--color-primary) that gives the active tab a distinctive "powered on"
look consistent with the dashboard's tech-focused aesthetic. DaisyUI's
default tab typography is sentence-case body font — clean and correct
for most apps, but doesn't match our visual language. Class composition
preserves both.

No CSS deletions this phase — .tab-btn / .tab-btn-active remain in
styles.css because they're not shadowing any DaisyUI class (DaisyUI uses
.tab, not .tab-btn, so they coexist).

Build verification:
  - npm run build: passes
  - DaisyUI .tabs, .tabs-border, .tab, .tab-active all present in built CSS

commits.subject: feat(btn): Phase 6 — migrate all buttons to DaisyUI btn (unshadow .btn-*)
commits.body:
Sixth of 10 phases from the DaisyUI component-class audit. Large but
mechanical migration — every button in the codebase now uses DaisyUI
`btn btn-*` classes and we delete ~200 lines of custom .btn-* CSS that
was shadowing DaisyUI's built-in button component.

Component changes:

main.jsx                 — error screen Retry:      btn-icon btn-primary -> btn btn-primary
Header.jsx               — filter toggle:           .filter-toggle.active -> btn btn-ghost btn-square btn-active
                         — filter badge:            .filter-badge        -> badge badge-primary badge-xs
                         — settings button:         .btn-theme           -> btn btn-ghost btn-square
HamburgerMenu.jsx        — menu trigger:            .btn-theme           -> btn btn-ghost btn-square
ShowMoreButton.jsx       — pagination button:       .show-more-btn       -> btn btn-ghost btn-block btn-sm mt-3
FilterSidebar.jsx        — Include/Exclude toggle:  .filter-mode-toggle  -> join wrapper + join-item btn btn-xs
                                                                            (btn-active btn-primary for include,
                                                                             btn-active btn-error for exclude)
                         — Clear All button:        .btn-icon .btn-secondary .filter-clear-btn -> btn btn-outline btn-sm w-full mt-3
DetailPane.jsx           — close button:            .detail-pane-close   -> btn btn-sm btn-circle btn-ghost
SettingsPane.jsx         — close button:            .settings-pane-close -> btn btn-sm btn-circle btn-ghost
Projects.jsx             — Open link:               .project-link .project-link-primary   -> btn btn-primary btn-xs
                         — GitHub link:             .project-link .project-link-secondary -> btn btn-outline btn-xs

CSS deletions (~200 lines):

.btn-icon / .btn-primary / .btn-primary:hover / .btn-secondary /
.btn-secondary:hover / .btn-icon svg — shadowed DaisyUI's built-in
.btn-primary / .btn-secondary

.btn-theme / .btn-theme:hover / .btn-theme svg — icon button style

.show-more-btn / .show-more-btn:hover — pagination button style

.filter-toggle / .filter-toggle:hover / .filter-toggle.active /
.filter-toggle svg — filter sidebar toggle

.filter-preset-btn / :hover / .active — unused (no JSX consumer)

.filter-mode-toggle + child button rules (5 variants) — Include/Exclude
segmented toggle

.filter-clear-btn — Clear All button

.detail-pane-close / :hover — detail pane close icon button

.settings-pane-close / :hover — settings pane close icon button

.project-link / .project-link-primary / :hover / .project-link-secondary /
:hover — project list action links

.filter-toggle .filter-badge — badge nested inside filter toggle button

Mobile responsive block — removed .btn-icon / .btn-theme / .filter-toggle
mobile-size override (40px -> 36px). DaisyUI btn-square is 40px on
desktop which is fine for mobile too; if crowding becomes an issue
again, use btn-sm conditionally on mobile breakpoints instead.

Focus-visible selector list — removed .btn-icon / .filter-mode-toggle
button / .filter-clear-btn from the custom focus ring list. DaisyUI
btn handles its own :focus-visible ring (via btn base styles), so those
entries would double-apply or conflict. Kept .tab-btn (Phase 7
migrates this), .collapsible-header (custom), .filter-multi-select-trigger
(Phase 10 stretch).

Print media query — replaced `.show-more-btn, .hamburger-menu, .modal`
hide rule with `[aria-label^="Show "].btn, .hamburger-menu, .modal`
— the show-more-btn class no longer exists; the attribute selector
matches the button via its aria-label prefix without needing .btn to
be too broad (would hide every button on the page when printing).

Build verification:
  - npm run build: passes, 84 modules
  - All DaisyUI btn classes present: btn, btn-primary, btn-ghost,
    btn-outline, btn-square, btn-circle, btn-sm, btn-xs, btn-block,
    btn-active, btn-error
  - All deleted custom classes confirmed GONE from built CSS:
    .btn-icon, .btn-theme, .show-more-btn, .filter-toggle, .filter-clear-btn,
    .filter-preset-btn, .filter-mode-toggle, .project-link-primary,
    .detail-pane-close, .settings-pane-close, .filter-badge

Impact on visuals:

- All buttons now get DaisyUI's default btn styling: slight subtle
  shadow on hover, active depression, disabled state, loading spinner
  slot, focus-visible ring.
- btn-square (40x40 default) matches our previous .btn-theme and
  .filter-toggle dimensions exactly.
- btn-ghost for icon buttons gives transparent background with hover
  tint — same visual as before.
- btn-primary / btn-outline replace our custom hover glow effects;
  DaisyUI's hover is a subtle background shift, not a glow. Slightly
  less flashy, more consistent with the rest of the DaisyUI ecosystem.
- Include/Exclude segmented toggle now uses the DaisyUI `join`
  component for connected border-radius instead of hand-rolled
  first-child/last-child rules.

commits.subject: feat(card): Phase 5 — migrate .card to DaisyUI card + card-body (unshadow)
commits.body:
Fifth of 10 phases from the DaisyUI component-class audit. This phase eliminates the .card shadow of DaisyUI's built-in card component by migrating all consumers and deleting the custom .card + related classes from styles.css. Changes: CollapsibleSection.jsx — the most impactful file in this phase because it wraps every dashboard section on every tab. Structure now:
...
...
card-body adds a DOM level but gives us DaisyUI's proper card structure. `p-6` (1.5rem) matches our previous 24px padding. `gap-0` overrides DaisyUI card-body's default 0.5rem flex gap which we don't want because our collapsible layout uses explicit margins. NOT migrating to DaisyUI's own `collapse` component — that's a stateless radio/checkbox mechanism that doesn't match React reducer state and doesn't support our chevron rotation / max-height transition. Documented as a rejected alternative in the component file. App.jsx — data-load error fallback migrated from .card to
The outer role="alert" was previously on the wrapper div (wrong element); moved onto the card itself where it semantically belongs. ErrorBoundary.jsx — section-level error fallback migrated the same way:
Projects.jsx — two migrations: 1. loadError fallback: .card projects-error -> card bg-base-200 + card-body items-center text-center py-8 2. ProjectCard component: .project-card -> card bg-base-200 border + card-body p-4 gap-0 (tighter padding than CollapsibleSection since project cards are denser, grid items) Discover.jsx — metric cards in the "Chew on this" section migrated from .card to card bg-base-200 border + card-body p-5 gap-0. styles.css — deleted ~35 lines: - .card / .card:hover / .card.cursor-pointer:hover (was shadowing DaisyUI's .card) - .card-loading (orphaned — no JSX consumer) - .projects-error (orphaned after the migration) - .project-card / .project-card:hover (migrated to DaisyUI card) - .error-boundary-card (orphaned) All replaced with one-line comments pointing at the component files. What's preserved: - 24px-ish visual padding via card-body's p-6/p-5/p-4 modifiers - bg-base-200 surface color - border-base-300 subtle border - hover:border-primary/40 transition on ProjectCard (explicit Tailwind) - collapsible-header + collapsible-content layout unchanged - All existing role="alert" / role="button" semantics What changed visually: - card-body adds an inner DOM layer (no visual effect) - The hover:border-color primary animation is no longer on .card by default — now only ProjectCard opts in via explicit hover:border-primary/40 - The `.card.cursor-pointer:hover { box-shadow: 0 0 20px ... }` glow effect from the old .card is not preserved — no current consumer needs it (Summary.jsx stat-card uses hover:ring-primary instead) Build verification: - npm run build: passes - DaisyUI .card and .card-body present in built CSS - .project-card, .projects-error, .error-boundary-card, .card-loading all confirmed GONE from built CSS Tags: ui, daisyui, refactor, card Complexity: 3 Urgency: 2 Impact: user-facing Risk: medium Debt: paid Epic: daisyui-component-migration Semver: minor https://claude.ai/code/session_018prUZYyhuCZQGS1azip9bG

commits.subject: feat(badges): Phase 4 — Timeline work-pattern badges to DaisyUI semantic variants
commits.body: Fourth of 10 phases from the DaisyUI component-class audit. This phase eliminates the .badge + .badge-holiday / .badge-weekend / .badge-after-hours shadow of DaisyUI's built-in badge component. Changes: Timeline.jsx — three conditional work-pattern badges switched from Holiday to Holiday Mapping: isHoliday -> badge-accent (warm pink-ish — festive) isWeekend -> badge-info (cool blue — informational) isAfterHours (not weekend) -> badge-warning (amber — "attention") badge-sm keeps the footprint compact inside the commit metadata row where the badges appear alongside author/date/repo text. styles.css — deleted ~20 lines of custom badge CSS: - .badge (base rule: inline-flex, padding, radius, font-size, uppercase, letter-spacing) — was SHADOWING DaisyUI's .badge - .badge-after-hours (rgba-amber) - .badge-weekend (rgba-purple) - .badge-holiday (rgba-pink) Replaced with a one-line comment pointing at Timeline.jsx. Why this matters beyond Timeline: Our .badge rule was unlayered CSS that won every specificity contest against DaisyUI's @layer components badge. Deleting it means any future badge usage anywhere in the codebase (e.g. a new contributor role badge in Contributors section, status badges on Projects, etc.) will correctly get DaisyUI's component styles without having to remember that "we shadow .badge". One less footgun. commits.subject: feat(health): Phase 3 — migrate security summary containers to DaisyUI alert
commits.body:
Third of 10 phases from the DaisyUI component-class audit. This phase converts the three "N security-related commits" summary containers in the Health section to DaisyUI `alert alert-error` with proper role="alert" ARIA semantics. Changes: Health.jsx — three summary containers (Phase-1 loading view, Executive view, Management view) switched from:
{count}
Security-related commits
to:
{count}
Security-related commits
Why: alert is a semantic notification component in both ARIA and DaisyUI. These containers tell the user "you have N security events to pay attention to" — the exact use case. With role="alert" screen readers announce the count immediately when the section renders; with `alert alert-error` we get DaisyUI's theme-aware error tint + `alert-content` text color pairing automatically, and we no longer need to hand-roll bg-error/10 + border-error/40 + text-error. Text colors changed from explicit text-base-content/80 and text-error to opacity-80 and opacity-60, inheriting from the alert-error `--alert-color` token so the text stays legible against the alert background regardless of the active DaisyUI theme (black, dim, coffee, dracula — each has its own error palette). The two individual-commit list-item containers (Phase-1 .slice(0,5) loop and Developer view securityCommits.map) stay as bespoke bg-error/10 divs — they're list items inside a security panel, not alerts themselves. Converting them to alert-error would produce three nested alerts per section, which is semantically wrong and visually noisy. flex flex-col items-center text-center layout is added inline because alert-error defaults to a row layout (icon slot + flex-1 message). Our summary containers stack the big number above the label, so we override to column with centered items. Build verification: - npm run build: passes - .alert-error class now present in built CSS Tags: ui, daisyui, a11y, refactor, alert Complexity: 1 Urgency: 1 Impact: user-facing Risk: low Debt: paid Epic: daisyui-component-migration Semver: patch https://claude.ai/code/session_018prUZYyhuCZQGS1azip9bG

commits.subject: feat(toast): Phase 2 — migrate Toast to DaisyUI toast + alert (unshadow .toast-*)
commits.body:
Second of 10 phases from the DaisyUI component-class audit. This phase
migrates the toast notification system to DaisyUI's `toast` positioning
wrapper + `alert alert-*` semantic variants, unshadowing the custom
.toast-* classes that were defined in styles.css and silently
overriding DaisyUI's built-in toast component.

Changes:

Toast.jsx — ToastItem now renders as `<div className="alert alert-${type}
shadow-lg transition-all duration-200 ease-out ...">` with the
type-to-class mapping in a small ALERT_CLASS_BY_TYPE object (success ->
alert-success, error -> alert-error, warning -> alert-warning, info ->
alert-info). Enter/exit animations are now Tailwind transition utilities
(opacity-0 translate-y-2 while exiting, opacity-100 translate-y-0
otherwise) instead of custom .toast-enter/.toast-exit keyframes. The
dismiss button is now `btn btn-ghost btn-xs btn-circle`. ToastContainer
uses `toast toast-bottom toast-center` (DaisyUI's positioning wrapper
classes) with an inline `style={{ zIndex: 'var(--z-toast)' }}` to pin
the stacking above other dashboard layers (DaisyUI's default z-index
doesn't stack above our debug pill or drawers).

styles.css — deleted ~65 lines of custom .toast-* CSS:
  - .toast-container (positioning)
  - .toast-item (layout, transitions)
  - .toast-enter / .toast-exit (animation states)
  - .toast-message
  - .toast-dismiss / .toast-dismiss:hover
  - .toast-success / .toast-error / .toast-info / .toast-warning
    (type colors that duplicated DaisyUI alert-* exactly)

All replaced with a one-line comment pointing at the component file.

Why this fixes the shadowing:

Our styles.css used to define .toast-container, .toast-item, etc.
UNLAYERED, which means they won ALL priority contests against DaisyUI's
own `.toast` / `.alert` definitions inside `@layer components`. Any
future `className="toast toast-top"` or `className="alert alert-error"`
usage elsewhere in the codebase would silently get our custom styles
instead of DaisyUI's component behavior. Deleting the custom classes
means DaisyUI's real `.toast` and `.alert` classes now apply, which is
what we actually want.

What's preserved:

- useToast() hook API and addToast/removeToast signatures — unchanged
- Auto-dismiss timer semantics (duration=0 means persistent)
- Two-stage isExiting state for animation-before-unmount ordering
- ARIA role="alert" for errors / role="status" for non-errors
- aria-live="assertive"/"polite" for screen reader urgency
- no-print class so toasts don't leak into PDF exports
- z-index: var(--z-toast) (= 70 from our scale, above drawers and menus)

Build verification:
  - npm test: 21 passing (no test regression)
  - npm run build: passes, 84 modules
  - DaisyUI .toast, .toast-bottom, .toast-center, .alert, .alert-success,
    .alert-error, .alert-warning, .alert-info, .alert-soft all present
    in built CSS
  - Old .toast-item / .toast-dismiss / .toast-message / .toast-enter /
    .toast-exit confirmed GONE from built CSS
  - Toast.jsx useToast API still referenced in the JS bundle

commits.subject: feat(modal): Phase 1 — migrate QuickGuide + InstallInstructionsModal to DaisyUI modal
commits.body:
First of 10 phases from the DaisyUI component-class audit. This phase
migrates the two self-contained modal components to DaisyUI's `modal`
component classes, eliminating ~35 custom CSS class definitions.

Changes:

QuickGuide.jsx — replaced bespoke .quick-guide-overlay + .quick-guide-modal
with DaisyUI's `<div className="modal modal-open">` + `<div className="modal-box">`
+ `<div className="modal-backdrop">` structure. Close button is now
`btn btn-sm btn-circle btn-ghost` (absolutely positioned with Tailwind
utilities). Skip/Next buttons are `btn btn-ghost btn-sm` / `btn btn-primary
btn-sm` in a `modal-action` footer. Pagination dots converted from
.quick-guide-dot to inline Tailwind (w-2 h-2 rounded-full bg-primary /
bg-base-300). Content text uses text-base-content / text-base-content/80
for the title/body hierarchy.

Modal-box width preserved via inline Tailwind (w-[min(420px,calc(100vw-32px))])
so the small-screen responsive cap still applies. Padding adjusted from
px-28-pt-32-pb-24 to the DaisyUI default with small overrides. Focus trap
and useEscapeKey hooks unchanged — they operate on the ref-held
container regardless of its class.

InstallInstructionsModal.jsx — same modal structure. The data-driven
numbered-step list is now a flex column with each step rendered as
<li class="flex items-start gap-3"> + a circular primary-tinted span for
the number. The optional warning note moves from .install-modal-note
to `<div role="alert" className="alert alert-warning alert-soft">` —
proper ARIA role AND DaisyUI alert component class. Benefits list keeps
its leading checkmarks as Unicode characters in the text (was a
::before pseudo-element before — simpler to put them in the JSX now
that the surrounding list is a flex column). Final "Got it" button is
`btn btn-primary btn-block` inside a modal-action wrapper.

styles.css — deleted two blocks (~130 lines total):
  - .quick-guide-overlay / .quick-guide-modal / .quick-guide-close /
    .quick-guide-content / .quick-guide-icon / .quick-guide-title /
    .quick-guide-body / .quick-guide-footer / .quick-guide-dots /
    .quick-guide-dot / .quick-guide-dot.active / .quick-guide-actions /
    .quick-guide-btn-primary / .quick-guide-btn-secondary + 3 keyframes
  - .install-modal-overlay / .install-modal-backdrop / .install-modal /
    .install-modal-header / .install-modal-steps / .install-modal-step /
    .install-modal-step-number / .install-modal-note /
    .install-modal-benefits / .install-modal-close

Each block replaced with a one-line comment pointing at the component
file so future contributors know why the class was removed.

Print override (@media print) — updated the "hide interactive chrome
when printing" rule from `.quick-guide-overlay, .quick-guide-modal` to
just `.modal` (DaisyUI's wrapper class, which now hosts both QuickGuide
and InstallInstructionsModal).

What's preserved:

- Focus trap via useFocusTrap — ref still threads through the modal-box
- Escape-to-close via useEscapeKey — unchanged
- Click-outside-to-close via onClick on .modal-backdrop — now a
  semantically-named DaisyUI class instead of custom .quick-guide-overlay
- Responsive width cap (min(420px, calc(100vw - 32px))) via inline
  Tailwind arbitrary value — matches the pre-migration visual
- Scroll lock via useScrollLock — unchanged (QuickGuide only)

Build + tests:
  - npm test: 21 passing, 0 failing, ~160 ms
  - npm run build: passes, 84 modules. CSS 160.06 → ~160 KB (tiny delta)
  - DaisyUI modal, modal-box, modal-backdrop, modal-action, modal-open
    classes all present in built CSS
  - All 4 QuickGuide step titles and all 3 install-instruction UI
    strings still present in built JS
  - All deleted custom classes confirmed GONE from built CSS

commits.subject: feat(theme): burger menu stays open during theme picker interactions
commits.body:
Closes the one actionable gap found in the glow-props comparison: our
burger menu closed after every item click, including theme picker clicks,
forcing users to reopen the menu to try a second theme. glow-props
deliberately omits the `data-close` attribute on theme controls so the
menu stays open — users can rapid-preview multiple themes in a single
menu session. Adopting the same UX.

Changes:

1. HamburgerMenu.handleItem() now takes the full item object and checks
   item.keepOpen. Two paths:

     - keepOpen: true → run the action immediately, skip close(), skip
       the 150 ms setTimeout. Used for theme picker items and the
       dark/light mode toggle so users can rapid-preview multiple themes
       or switch modes then pick a theme without reopening the menu.

     - default → close the menu first, then run the action after 150 ms
       (matches the CSS fade animation so state changes from the action
       don't cause visual glitches while the menu is fading out). Used
       for Quick Guide, User Guide, Save as PDF, Install App, Update Now.

   Shared error handling extracted into a runAction(action) helper so
   both paths route errors through debugAdd('render', 'error', ...).

   Call site updated from handleItem(item.action) to handleItem(item).
   handleItem is internal to HamburgerMenu; no other callers.

   JSDoc updated to document the new keepOpen item field.

2. Header.jsx menuItems memo now sets keepOpen: true on:

     - The dark/light mode toggle item. Users can toggle modes and then
       pick a theme for the new mode in a single menu session. The theme
       list below the toggle swaps to the new mode's themes when the
       toggle action dispatches.

     - All 4 theme picker items for the current mode. Clicking Nord →
       theme applies, menu stays open, Nord is now marked active with
       checkmark. Clicking Emerald → theme applies, menu stays open,
       Emerald is now marked active. All 4 themes previewable in 4 clicks
       from a single menu open.

   Other menu items (Quick Guide, User Guide, Save as PDF, Install App,
   Update Now) keep their existing close-then-act behavior — only theme
   controls got keepOpen.

Why this is safe:

- Menu items are keyed by item.label in HamburgerMenu's render loop, and
  theme names are stable across re-renders. When activeThemeId changes
  after a picker click, React reconciles the buttons in place — the
  clicked button's DOM element is preserved, focus stays on it, only
  icon/highlight/ariaLabel props update.

- The ariaLabel change ("Use Nord theme (Cool blue-gray)" → "Use Nord
  theme (Cool blue-gray), currently active") provides natural
  screen-reader feedback confirming the theme was applied.

- click-outside, Escape, and the backdrop click handler all still work
  — keepOpen only suppresses the per-item auto-close. Users can always
  dismiss the menu.

- The 150 ms setTimeout path still applies to non-keepOpen items so
  close-animation-vs-action ordering is preserved for those.

Things from glow-props deliberately NOT adopted (documented in docs/HISTORY.md):

- 35-theme catalog. Our curated 4+4 is the right tradeoff for a utility
  app per the reference doc's own advice.
- Random theme on load. Wrong for an analytics dashboard.
- Auto-classify from color-scheme. Only helps with full catalogs; we
  already validate against DaisyUI's classification.
- Regex-based multi-file rewrite. Fragile to reformatting.
- Manual generator invocation. We prebuild for dev/build/build:lib.

Build and tests:
- npm test: 21 passing, 0 failing, 6 suites, ~145 ms
- npm run build: passes, 84 modules. CSS 160.06 KB (unchanged — no
  CSS change this pass). JS 555.75 → 556.03 KB (+0.28 KB for the
  refactored handleItem logic + keepOpen: true literals).
- Generator idempotent — all 4 downstream files report "unchanged"
- Smoke test via vite preview + curl verified: keepOpen identifier
  survived minification into the JS bundle; all 8 theme names,
  descriptions, aria-labels, CSS theme selectors, and flash
  prevention hex values still present; all 11 critical custom class
  families still emitted.

Documentation updated:
- docs/HISTORY.md — full entry with rationale for every not-adopted item
- docs/SESSION_NOTES.md — sixth pass summary at the top
- docs/TESTING_GUIDE.md — 4 new test scenarios: rapid-preview behavior,
  mode toggle keepOpen, focus preservation after theme click, non-theme
  items still close the menu
- docs/USER_GUIDE.md — updated Theme section to mention rapid-preview

commits.subject: feat(theme): canva-grid alignment — oklchToHex module+tests, COLOR_KEY_OVERRIDES, debug flow tracing, Approach A/B docs
commits.body:
After cross-referencing our theming against canva-grid's implementation
(the sibling project using "Approach B" named combos), five patterns
from canva-grid were worth adopting without changing our Approach A
storage shape. All implemented this pass.

1. Extracted scripts/oklchToHex.mjs as a standalone module with 21 unit
   tests via Node's built-in node:test runner (no Jest dependency —
   node:test has been stable since Node 20, we're on Node 22). Tests
   cover CSS Color Level 4 edge cases (boundary values, achromatic
   colors, optional hue, alpha channel, whitespace, out-of-gamut
   clamping, invalid input) and DaisyUI fixtures (nord primary,
   autumn primary, dracula base-100, lofi base-300). npm test runs
   the suite in ~150 ms.

2. Fixed the L=1 percentage-vs-decimal edge case the old inlined
   version had. Old heuristic: `if (L > 1) L /= 100`. Couldn't
   distinguish `oklch(1 0 0)` (decimal, meaning white) from
   `oklch(1% 0 0)` (percent, near-black). New regex captures the
   percent character explicitly: `([\d.]+)(%?)` and only divides by
   100 when isPercent === true. Three dedicated tests pin the fix.
   DaisyUI always uses percent form so this never triggered in
   practice, but the fix is free and matches the CSS spec.

3. Added COLOR_KEY_OVERRIDES to scripts/generate-theme-meta.mjs.
   Default rule now matches the reference:
     - Light themes: --color-primary (the brand accent)
     - Dark themes: --color-base-100 (the main surface)
   With overrides for monochrome/warm-minimal light themes whose
   primary is near-black:
     - lofi → --color-base-300 (→ #ebebeb, from canva-grid)
     - caramellatte → --color-base-300 (→ #ffd6a7, our addition —
       DaisyUI ships caramellatte with primary = oklch(0% 0 0)
       literal black, a DaisyUI design decision)

   New PWA status bar colors:
     lofi          light  #ebebeb  base-300  (was #ffffff)
     nord          light  #5e81ac  primary   (was #ffffff)
     emerald       light  #66cc8a  primary   (was #ffffff)
     caramellatte  light  #ffd6a7  base-300  (was #fff7ed)
     black         dark   #000000  base-100  (unchanged)
     dim           dark   #2a303c  base-100  (unchanged)
     coffee        dark   #261b25  base-100  (unchanged)
     dracula       dark   #282a36  base-100  (unchanged)

   Light themes now have theme-appropriate brand colors instead of
   uniform white. Mobile users see a distinct status bar per theme.

4. Added theme-change flow tracing via debugLog. New `theme` source
   color (lavender #c4b5fd) in DebugPill SOURCE_COLORS so theme
   events are visually distinct from render errors and boot events
   in the debug pill's log tab. themes.js imports debugAdd from
   debugLog.js (zero-import module, no circular dependency risk)
   and calls logThemeEvent('theme-applied', {dark, requested,
   validated, skipPersist}) at the end of every applyTheme()
   invocation. `requested !== validated` signals a stale or
   cross-mode theme id was dispatched. Every theme-apply path gets
   logged once: mount, toggle, picker click, cross-tab event, embed
   override. Pattern from canva-grid's useDarkMode hook.

5. Documented Approach A vs B tradeoff in CLAUDE.md Dashboard
   Architecture section. We use Approach A (per-mode independent,
   3 storage keys: darkMode/lightTheme/darkTheme) with 4 light + 4
   dark = 16 possible (mode, theme) combinations. canva-grid and
   other sibling projects use Approach B (named combos, 2 keys:
   darkMode/themeCombo) with typically 2-5 combo options. Our
   rationale: the dashboard is a utility app with no brand-coupled
   theme pairings, so constraining users to pre-vetted combos is
   unnecessarily restrictive compared to independent per-mode
   selection. Tradeoff: slightly more complex picker UX (two
   clicks: mode then theme) vs canva-grid's single-click combo
   buttons.

Deliberately NOT adopted from canva-grid (documented rationale):

- Combo-based persistence (Approach B). Structural change that
  would lose per-mode-independent theme choice. Conscious decision.
- Theme picker in page header instead of burger menu. canva-grid's
  inline join component is more discoverable but our dashboard
  header is already crowded with tab nav, filter controls, and
  settings. Burger menu placement is a deliberate UX decision.
- Regex-based index.html rewrite. canva-grid uses
  html.replace(/var combos = [^;]+;/, ...) which silently fails on
  reformat. Our marker-based BEGIN/END GENERATED approach fails
  loudly on missing markers and survives reformatting.
- Manual npm run generate-theme-meta. canva-grid's generator runs
  only on-demand. Ours runs as the first step of every dev/build/
  build:lib so drift is impossible.

Files changed:

New:
- scripts/oklchToHex.mjs (standalone module, improved parsing)
- scripts/__tests__/oklchToHex.test.mjs (21 tests)

Modified:
- scripts/generate-theme-meta.mjs (import oklchToHex, add
  COLOR_KEY_OVERRIDES, per-theme colorKey in collected metadata,
  richer stdout report)
- dashboard/js/themes.js (import debugAdd, logThemeEvent helper,
  call from applyTheme)
- dashboard/js/components/DebugPill.jsx (add theme source color)
- dashboard/js/generated/themeMeta.js (regenerated hex values)
- dashboard/index.html (regenerated flash prevention META map)
- package.json (add "test" script)
- CLAUDE.md (Approach A vs B section)
- docs/HISTORY.md + docs/SESSION_NOTES.md (full chronology)

Build + test:
- npm test: 21 passing, 0 failing, 6 suites
- npm run build: passes, 84 modules, CSS 157.40 → 160.06 KB
  (+2.66 KB from new SOURCE_COLORS entry scanned into Tailwind)
- Generator idempotent: second run reports all 4 files unchanged
- themes.js loads cleanly via Node direct import with browser stub,
  applyTheme() runs through debugAdd path without throwing
- All 8 [data-theme=...] selectors present in built CSS
- All 8 new hex values present in inline flash prevention META map
- All 15 critical custom class families still emitted

Side catch: tried to write `theme/*/object.js` inside a JSDoc block
comment in the test file during initial draft. The */ sequence inside
the path terminated the block comment early and produced a Node syntax
error. CLAUDE.md prohibits this and AI_MISTAKES has a 2026-04-12 entry
for the same mistake. Fixed by converting the header to // line
comments with a note explaining why. This is the third time this
mistake happened in this session — every future block comment should
avoid filesystem paths containing stars.

commits.subject: fix(theme): self-audit cleanup — 3 bugs + 2 dead code + 1 optimization
commits.body:
Self-audit pass that caught findings the previous passes missed. Six real
issues plus documentation for two known quirks and test coverage gaps.

1. Bug: themes.js __allThemes / __isDark exports referenced undefined
   THEME_NAMES / IS_DARK after an earlier pass removed those imports.
   Rollup tree-shook them out of the browser bundle (nothing imports
   them) so the dashboard worked, but `node --input-type=module -e
   "import('./dashboard/js/themes.js')"` threw ReferenceError. Deleted
   the dead debug-helper exports — they were speculative "exposed for
   ad-hoc console inspection" helpers with zero consumers, violating
   CLAUDE.md's "Don't design for hypothetical future requirements."

2. Bug: persistTheme left stale lightTheme / darkTheme keys on
   revert-to-default. Example: user picks Nord then reverts to Lo-Fi
   (default). Old code wrote `darkMode=false` and SKIPPED the lightTheme
   write. lightTheme='nord' stayed in localStorage. On next reload,
   flash prevention applied Nord — the user's most recent pick was lost.
   Fix: safeStorageRemove() the per-mode key on revert-to-default instead
   of skipping the write. Added safeStorageRemove to the themes.js
   imports. Documented the "remove key means revert to default" contract
   in the function comment.

3. Bug: cross-tab listener filtered e.newValue === null events. Old
   handler had `if (e.key === 'lightTheme' && e.newValue)` which treated
   null as "nothing to do". But fix #2 now removes keys on revert, firing
   storage events with newValue=null — other tabs need to see that and
   revert their reducer state to the default. Fix: drop the && e.newValue
   filter; validLightTheme / validDarkTheme already handle null → default
   via the .has(id) check failing. Symmetric darkMode key removal falls
   back to matchMedia. Comment rewritten to document the contract.

4. Dead code: getStoredTheme(dark) in themes.js — defined, exported,
   never called anywhere (grep confirmed). Holdover from an earlier pass
   where the darkMode effect called applyTheme(state.darkMode,
   getStoredTheme(state.darkMode)) — replaced with direct reducer state
   reads. Deleted the function entirely.

5. Dead code: getStoredTheme import in AppContext.jsx — same reason.
   Removed from the import line.

6. Clarity: loadInitialState matchMedia fallback now reads matchMedia
   directly instead of document.documentElement.classList.contains('dark').
   The old code relied on the flash prevention script having set the
   class from the same matchMedia query — functionally equivalent but
   indirect. Reading matchMedia directly keeps the function independent
   of DOM side effects and makes it unit-testable without a DOM.

7. Optimization: darkMode useEffect dependency narrowed from
   [darkMode, lightTheme, darkTheme] to [darkMode, activeTheme].
   activeTheme is derived outside the effect as `state.darkMode ?
   state.darkTheme : state.lightTheme`. With the old deps, a cross-tab
   event that updated the NON-active mode's theme triggered a no-op
   effect re-run with wasteful getComputedStyle + Chart.js defaults
   write. With the new deps the effect only re-runs when the
   currently-applied mode's theme changes.

End-to-end verification (new this pass):

Added a Node-based smoke test that stubs localStorage, window, document,
getComputedStyle, dynamically imports themes.js, and exercises:
  - Catalog shape (4 light + 4 dark { id, name, description } objects)
  - DEFAULT_LIGHT_THEME / DEFAULT_DARK_THEME constants
  - Validators: null, undefined, unknown, cross-mode, valid inputs
  - persistTheme: write on non-default, REMOVE on revert-to-default
  - Approach A per-mode persistence for both light and dark
This test caught bug #2 during the audit before any rebuild.

All tests pass. Output excerpt:
  After persistTheme(false, nord):       lightTheme = nord
  After persistTheme(false, lofi) revert: lightTheme = undefined (removed)
  After persistTheme(true, dracula):  darkTheme = dracula
  After persistTheme(true, black):    darkTheme = undefined (removed)

Documentation:

- CLAUDE.md: architecture line for js/themes.js updated — removed
  getStoredTheme mention, documented persistTheme / getMetaColor.
- docs/HISTORY.md: new entry with full finding list + rationale.
- docs/SESSION_NOTES.md: new "Known quirks" section documenting
  mount-time darkMode persistence (limits OS fallback to first paint,
  pre-existing, consistent with reference pattern's unconditional
  persistTheme — not fixed to avoid scope creep). New "Test coverage
  gaps" section explicitly documenting that browser runtime testing
  was NOT executed this session (only vite preview + curl + Node
  reducer simulation) — next session should run in a real browser.
- docs/TESTING_GUIDE.md: added simulated cross-tab test with
  copy-pasteable DevTools console snippet (StorageEvent dispatch),
  invalid-payload safety test, and explicit "revert to default removes
  key" test so two-tab access isn't required for verification.

Build: passes. CSS 157.40 KB (unchanged), JS 555.83 KB (+0.08 KB).
84 modules. Generator idempotent (all 4 files "unchanged" on rebuild).
themes.js now loads cleanly via `node --input-type=module` — no
ReferenceError. All 8 theme names, descriptions, selectors, and 11
critical custom class families still present in the bundle.

Urgency: 3

commits.subject: feat(theme): single-source config + 4x4 catalog + burger-menu picker + reference-shape cross-tab handler
commits.body:
Closes all seven residual gaps against docs/implementations/THEME_DARK_MODE.md
documented in the previous "differences" audit. The user asked for "no
shortcuts, no skipping", so this pass implements every actionable gap and
explicitly documents why the two remaining items would be speculative
abstraction (CLAUDE.md prohibition).

Gaps closed:

1. (C#1) Theme catalog was string arrays — now [{ id, name, description }, ...]
   per reference. Validator sets rebuilt via .map(t => t.id).

2. (C#2 + C#3) Generator only rewrote themeMeta.js — now rewrites FOUR files
   from a single source (scripts/theme-config.js) via BEGIN/END GENERATED
   marker blocks:
     - dashboard/js/generated/themeMeta.js (full rewrite)
     - dashboard/js/themes.js theme-catalog block
     - dashboard/styles.css daisyui-plugin block
     - dashboard/index.html flash-prevention-meta block
   Each write is content-equality checked so idempotent reruns don't bump
   mtimes / trigger Vite HMR. Validates config at load time: fails fast on
   invalid defaults, wrong color-scheme classification, or nonexistent
   DaisyUI theme ids.

3. (C#5) Cross-tab handler filtered theme events by current mode — now
   dispatches unconditionally for darkMode / lightTheme / darkTheme keys,
   matching the reference's setIsDark/setLightThemeState/setDarkThemeState
   shape. AppContext reducer gains lightTheme/darkTheme state with no-op
   early-return guards to skip re-renders when cross-tab events replay
   the current value.

4. (C#7) No theme picker UI — new menu items in Header.jsx filtered to
   the current mode via LIGHT_THEMES / DARK_THEMES. Active theme gets
   the highlight class + checkmark icon, inactive themes get a palette
   icon. Each item has an explicit ariaLabel like "Use Nord theme
   (Cool blue-gray), currently active".

5. Catalog expanded from 1+1 to 4+4 curated themes (prerequisite for C#7):
     light: lofi / nord / emerald / caramellatte
     dark:  black / dim / coffee / dracula
   All DaisyUI stock — no custom theme definitions. Sits inside the
   reference's "2-5 curated combos (or 8-10 themes per mode)" range.

6. Generator fail-fast validation (new this pass) — error paths tested:
     - Invalid DEFAULT_*_THEME          -> error + exit 1
     - Nonexistent theme id             -> error + exit 1
     - Wrong color-scheme classification -> error + exit 1

7. setTheme(themeName) helper — new useAppDispatch() export that infers
   which mode the theme belongs to via validLightTheme/validDarkTheme and
   dispatches SET_LIGHT_THEME or SET_DARK_THEME. Unknown ids silently
   ignored (reducer guards prevent garbage state).

Deliberately NOT addressed (speculative abstraction per CLAUDE.md):

- (C#4) CSP hash for inline flash prevention script. No CSP deployed,
  computing a hash that nothing verifies is dead code. Revisit when
  CSP is added to vercel.json.

- (C#6) Legacy localStorage key cleanup (theme / colorMode / dark-mode).
  Our pre-DaisyUI system used the same darkMode key, nothing to clean up.
  Adding a defensive safeStorageRemove would be a no-op runtime cost.

New files:
- scripts/theme-config.js

Rewritten:
- scripts/generate-theme-meta.mjs (reads theme-config.js, propagates to 4 files,
  idempotent writes, fail-fast validation)

Modified:
- dashboard/js/themes.js (BEGIN/END markers, object-shape catalog, updated
  validators, removed obsolete "currently one theme per mode" caveat)
- dashboard/styles.css (BEGIN/END markers around @plugin "daisyui" block)
- dashboard/index.html (BEGIN/END markers inside inline flash prevention script)
- dashboard/js/AppContext.jsx (lightTheme/darkTheme reducer state, new action
  types + guards, darkMode effect reads from reducer state, unconditional
  cross-tab dispatch, setTheme helper via dispatch context)
- dashboard/js/components/Header.jsx (imports LIGHT_THEMES/DARK_THEMES, new
  palette/check icons, menuItems memo builds theme picker for current mode)
- dashboard/js/generated/themeMeta.js (regenerated with 8 themes' hex values)

Docs:
- CLAUDE.md (architecture list: theme-config.js + expanded generator description)
- docs/SESSION_NOTES.md + docs/HISTORY.md (full chronology, gap-by-gap resolution)
- docs/TODO.md (collapsed — only optional component-class migration left)
- docs/TESTING_GUIDE.md (generator tests, error path tests, idempotence
  cross-check, theme picker tests, "add a new theme" developer flow)
- docs/USER_GUIDE.md (theme picker explanation with all 8 themes)

Build: passes. CSS bundle 150.64 KB -> 157.40 KB (+6.76 KB, +4.5%) from 6
additional DaisyUI theme CSS-variable blocks (each ~1 KB, expected and
unavoidable). 84 modules transform. Only CSS warning is DaisyUI's internal
@property --radialprogress (not ours).

Smoke test via vite preview + curl:
- Inline HTML flash prevention script: all 4+4 theme ids + 8 hex values
- Built CSS: all 8 [data-theme="..."] selectors (lofi..dracula)
- Built JS: all 8 theme names and descriptions (Lo-Fi/Minimal monochrome
  through Dracula/Dev classic)
- Generator idempotent: second run reports all 4 files "unchanged"
- All 9 critical custom class families survived the CSS grow

commits.subject: refactor(theme): align with THEME_DARK_MODE reference — catalog module, applyTheme helper, meta generator, allowlist
commits.body:
After the full DaisyUI migration landed on this branch, a side-by-side
comparison with docs/implementations/THEME_DARK_MODE.md surfaced seven
divergences from the reference pattern. None were bugs that would
immediately manifest, but all were the kind of structural drift that
decays into bugs the moment someone adds a theme or changes a default.
This pass closes the gap on every actionable divergence.

Divergences addressed:

1. No theme catalog module — theme names and meta colors were hardcoded
   module constants at the top of AppContext.jsx.
2. No applyTheme() helper — the same dual-layer DOM mutation (.dark +
   data-theme + meta theme-color + Chart.js defaults) was inlined in
   three places (AppContext darkMode effect, App.jsx embed override,
   inline flash prevention script). Any drift between them = visual bugs.
3. PWA meta theme-color hex values hardcoded — reference wants a build
   script that reads DaisyUI's theme/*/object.js and converts oklch to
   hex at build time so the values never drift.
4. No theme ID allowlist in flash prevention script — a removed theme
   ID in localStorage would produce an unstyled first paint.
5. Cross-tab storage listener only handled darkMode — no forward-compat
   for lightTheme/darkTheme keys a theme picker would write.
6. No explicit aria-label on the theme toggle menu item — the visible
   label "Light mode"/"Dark mode" describes a destination, not an action.
7. No getStoredTheme/persistTheme/validLightTheme/validDarkTheme helpers
   exposed as part of a catalog module.

New files:

scripts/generate-theme-meta.mjs — imports each registered DaisyUI theme's
  object.js via dynamic import(), runs an inlined oklch->hex converter
  (oklch -> oklab -> linear sRGB via LMS -> gamma-corrected sRGB -> hex,
  ~30 lines, no color library dependency), and writes
  dashboard/js/generated/themeMeta.js with META_COLORS, IS_DARK, and
  THEME_NAMES exports. Validated against known values: lofi base-100
  oklch(100% 0 0) -> #ffffff, black base-100 oklch(0% 0 0) -> #000000.

dashboard/js/themes.js — theme catalog module. Exports LIGHT_THEMES,
  DARK_THEMES, DEFAULT_LIGHT_THEME, DEFAULT_DARK_THEME, validLightTheme,
  validDarkTheme, getStoredTheme, persistTheme, getMetaColor, and most
  importantly applyTheme(dark, themeName, skipPersist) — the single
  source of truth for .dark class + data-theme attribute + meta
  theme-color content + Chart.js defaults. Every theme-affecting caller
  now routes through this function. Imports META_COLORS/IS_DARK/
  THEME_NAMES from ./generated/themeMeta.js and Chart from chart.js
  (ES modules cache the Chart singleton shared with main.jsx and
  AppContext.jsx).

dashboard/js/generated/themeMeta.js — auto-generated, committed so fresh
  clones work without running the generator first. Header says DO NOT
  EDIT BY HAND. Regenerated by the prebuild hook on every npm run dev
  and npm run build.

Refactored:

dashboard/js/AppContext.jsx — removed LIGHT_THEME/DARK_THEME/
  LIGHT_META_COLOR/DARK_META_COLOR module constants and the direct
  `import { Chart as ChartJS } from 'chart.js'` (both moved to
  themes.js). darkMode useEffect collapsed from 30 lines of inlined DOM
  mutations to a single applyTheme(state.darkMode,
  getStoredTheme(state.darkMode)) call. Cross-tab storage listener now
  handles three keys — darkMode dispatches to the reducer, lightTheme
  and darkTheme call applyTheme(..., skipPersist=true) directly when
  the key matches the current mode.

dashboard/js/App.jsx — imports applyTheme + DEFAULT_*_THEME from
  themes.js. Embed override ?theme=light|dark calls applyTheme with
  skipPersist=true instead of doing its own classList + setAttribute
  dance. ?bg=... still sets --color-base-100 directly since it's a
  pure surface override, not a theme swap.

dashboard/index.html — inline flash prevention script gained
  LIGHT_THEMES/DARK_THEMES allowlists, a validTheme() helper, and a
  META map inline. Reads darkTheme/lightTheme from localStorage in
  addition to darkMode, validates each, falls back to defaults on
  miss. Applies .dark class + data-theme attribute + meta theme-color
  content before first paint. Script comment documents the four-place
  sync requirement (this script + themes.js + styles.css @plugin
  config + generate-theme-meta.mjs REGISTERED_THEMES).

dashboard/js/components/Header.jsx — theme toggle menu item gained an
  explicit ariaLabel: state.darkMode ? 'Switch to light mode' :
  'Switch to dark mode'. Visible label stays the same ("Light mode" /
  "Dark mode"). Comment explains why the destination-style label needs
  an action-style aria-label.

dashboard/js/components/HamburgerMenu.jsx — accepts an optional
  item.ariaLabel prop on menu items, applied as the button's aria-label
  attribute. Falls back to item.label for menu items that don't need
  a distinct screen-reader label.

package.json — dev, build, and build:lib scripts all prefix with
  `node scripts/generate-theme-meta.mjs &&` so the generated file is
  always fresh. Added `npm run generate-theme-meta` for manual
  invocation after adding themes.

Not addressed (feature decisions, not bugs):

- Approach A vs Approach B storage schema. This project has one theme
  per mode and no picker UI, so neither approach is "chosen" — we ship
  Approach A's key schema (darkMode + lightTheme + darkTheme) as a
  superset with the extra keys being forward-compat plumbing only.
  Documented in themes.js top comment.
- Theme picker UI in burger menu — tracked as optional follow-up in
  docs/TODO.md. Infrastructure (catalog, cross-tab sync, allowlist,
  persistTheme) is all in place.
- DaisyUI component classes instead of custom CSS — already tracked
  as optional follow-up. Separate incremental pass from theming.

Build: passes. CSS bundle 150.64 KB (unchanged — no CSS changes this
pass, only JS + HTML refactoring). 84 modules transform (up from 82
because themes.js and generated/themeMeta.js are new). Only CSS warning
is DaisyUI's internal @property --radialprogress (not ours).

Smoke test via vite preview + curl verified:
- HTML <html lang="en" class="dark" data-theme="black">
- Both <meta name="theme-color"> tags with media queries
- Inline flash prevention script contains LIGHT_THEMES, DARK_THEMES,
  'lofi': '#ffffff', 'black': '#000000', validTheme
- Built CSS contains both [data-theme="lofi"] and [data-theme="black"]
- All 14 critical custom class families present (.heatmap-0..4,
  .heatmap-cell, .filter-multi-select*, .settings-pane*, .detail-pane*,
  .error-boundary-card, .dashboard-header, .card, .btn-primary,
  .toast-success)

Grep verification: themes.js is the only file that touches
classList.add/remove('dark'), setAttribute('data-theme', ...), or
ChartJS.defaults. Zero duplication.
